primary_diagnosis.name: Interstitial cystitis / bladder pain syndrome — acute flare with pelvic-floor spasm

pathophysiology_summary: IC/BPS involves a sensitised bladder and pelvic-floor musculature with neurogenic inflammation producing suprapubic pain on filling/voiding, urinary frequency/urgency, allodynia and pelvic-floor spasm; severe pain drives autonomic arousal (tachycardia/tachypnoea). The presentation mimics complicated UTI/pyelonephritis, obstructing calculus and acute retention, but afebrile status, absent flank pain, continued small-volume voiding and negative nitrites/leukocytes point to a sterile flare. Because telehealth cannot examine the abdomen, test urine, or scan the bladder, these not-to-miss diagnoses are excluded by targeted history plus in-person urinalysis, and strong sedatives are not initiated remotely in an overdose-risk patient who has already maxed OTC analgesia.

differential.diagnosis: Interstitial cystitis / bladder pain syndrome flare
differential.evidence_basis: Known IC/BPS with a classic suprapubic pain/frequency flare.
Dysuria/frequency demand screening for fever, flank pain and urinalysis.
Severe urinary pain warrants screening for loin-to-groin colic and haematuria.
Voiding only drops raises retention; continued small volumes argue against it.

differential.diagnosis: Interstitial cystitis / bladder pain syndrome flare
differential.evidence_basis: Afebrile, no flank pain, still voiding, negative nitrites/leukocytes — a sterile flare, pending MCS.
Lower probability but not fully excludable without culture.
No loin-to-groin colic; strictly suprapubic pain.
Continued voiding of small volumes excludes complete retention.

differential.diagnosis: Interstitial cystitis / bladder pain syndrome flare
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: sterile IC/BPS flare; confirm with in-person urinalysis/MCS and manage pain safely without remote sedatives.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Upper-tract infection can progress to urosepsis and requires prompt antibiotics.

red_flags.description: Pain shifting to the mid-back/flank suggesting obstruction/stone
red_flags.clinical_significance: Obstructing calculus with infection is a urological emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Obstructed infected system, sepsis, renal damage.

red_flags.description: Completely stopping passing urine (>6h) or frank clots/gross haematuria
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute retention or clot retention needs urgent catheterisation.

red_flags.description: Overdose risk — has maxed OTC analgesia and is desperate
red_flags.clinical_significance: Catastrophising with maxed analgesia risks intentional/accidental overdose.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting the known IC label without screening infection/obstruction/retention that cannot be excluded remotely.
Prescribing diazepam/amitriptyline stat to an overdose-risk patient who has maxed OTC analgesia.
Not recognising the maxed OTC analgesia and catastrophising as an overdose hazard.
Attributing tachycardia/tachypnoea solely to pain without confirming afebrile status and excluding sepsis.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Low likelihood of upper-tract infection on history (afebrile, no CVA pain, negative dipstick) — culture pending
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports in-person urinalysis and safe symptomatic care rather than remote sedation or empirical antibiotics.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen for infection/obstruction/retention, arrange prompt in-person urinalysis and assessment, provide safe supportive measures, and escalate to ED for fever/flank pain, stopping passing urine, or clots.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Sedatives/tricyclics prescribed over chat could harm an overdose-risk patient and mask an evolving infection or obstruction.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: IC/BPS is a diagnosis of exclusion requiring negative urine culture; flares are managed with multimodal non-opioid measures.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP guidance on urinary tract infection
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Dysuria requires urinalysis and, where indicated, culture; upper-tract features warrant urgent assessment.
